Description: S3 encryption configurations — from default SSE-S3 to customer-managed KMS keys

Example1_DefaultSSE_S3.Description: Default encryption with Amazon S3-managed keys (SSE-S3) — simplest option, zero cost, recommended as baseline

Example2_SSE_KMS_AWSManagedKey.Description: Encryption with AWS-managed KMS key (aws/s3) — adds CloudTrail audit of key usage

Example3_SSE_KMS_CustomerManagedKey.Description: Encryption with customer-managed KMS key (CMK) — full control over key policies, rotation, and access. Required for strict compliance

KMS_Key_Policy.Description: The KMS key policy must grant the S3 service access to use the key

SSE-KMS (AWS managed key).Key Management: AWS manages key, you see it in KMS
SSE-KMS (AWS managed key).Audit Trail: Yes — every use logged in CloudTrail
SSE-KMS (AWS managed key).Best For: Audit requirements without key management overhead

SSE-KMS (Customer managed key).Key Management: You manage key policies, rotation, grants

SSE-C.Key Management: Customer provides key with every request
SSE-C.Best For: Rare — when you must control the key entirely

AWS_CLI_Commands.UploadWithKMS: aws s3 cp file.txt s3://my-bucket/ --sse aws:kms --sse-kms-key-id alias/my-key

InterviewTip: BucketKeyEnabled is a key optimization — it reduces KMS API calls (and cost) by 99% by generating a bucket-level key from your KMS key. Always enable it with SSE-KMS. Since Jan 2023, S3 automatically encrypts all new objects with SSE-S3 by default — but you should still explicitly configure encryption to enforce your chosen algorithm via bucket policy.
